---
title: "Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis"
description: "Geospatial fuzzy matching of 3,944 Tanzanian administrative wards across a boundary change, Monte Carlo proof of collider bias, and choropleth mapping of U.S. animal welfare enforcement."
date: 2026-04-26
image: "../assets/project-stata8-thumb.png"
toc: true
---

```{=html}
<div style="display:flex;gap:0.5rem;flex-wrap:wrap;margin-bottom:1.5rem;">
  <span class="pill pill-blue">Fuzzy matching</span>
  <span class="pill pill-sky">ArcGIS · spmap</span>
  <span class="pill pill-teal">Causal inference</span>
  <span class="pill pill-slate">Choropleth mapping</span>
</div>

<div class="proj-infobox">
  <div style="display:flex;gap:2rem;flex-wrap:wrap;">
    <div><div class="proj-label">Course</div><div class="proj-value">PPOL 6818 · Georgetown University</div></div>
    <div><div class="proj-label">Data scale</div><div class="proj-value">3,944 wards · 1,200 simulations</div></div>
    <div><div class="proj-label">Tools</div><div class="proj-value">Stata, ArcGIS, spmap, shp2dta</div></div>
    <div><div class="proj-label">GitHub</div><div class="proj-value"><a href="https://github.com/gui2de/ppol6818-spring2026/pull/201/changes" target="_blank">View code ↗</a></div></div>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Part 1: Geospatial fuzzy matching — Tanzania ward redistricting (2010–2015)

Tanzania's ward count grew from **3,333 to 3,944** between 2010 and 2015 through administrative subdivision. Matching electoral datasets across this boundary change required reconciling two non-overlapping geographic classifications — a standard problem in any longitudinal sub-national policy analysis.

**Method:** Used ArcGIS shapefile intersection data (2012 and 2017 shapefiles) to compute the percentage area overlap between each 2015 ward and all candidate 2010 parent wards. Applied a 50% area-overlap threshold to classify parent-child relationships.

```{=html}
<div class="grid-4" style="margin:1rem 0;">
  <div class="stat-card">
    <div class="stat-number">2,705</div>
    <div class="stat-label">Stable wards (unchanged 2010–2015)</div>
  </div>
  <div class="stat-card">
    <div class="stat-number">488</div>
    <div class="stat-label">Split into exactly 2 new wards</div>
  </div>
  <div class="stat-card stat-card-teal">
    <div class="stat-number">45</div>
    <div class="stat-label">Split into 3 or more wards</div>
  </div>
  <div class="stat-card stat-card-sky">
    <div class="stat-number">440 / 616</div>
    <div class="stat-label">Parentless / orphan wards</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

**Top regions by ward division rate:**

| Region | Division rate | Context |
|---|---|---|
| Rukwa | 39.7% | Highest — major administrative restructuring |
| Morogoro | 34.7% | |
| Katavi | 28.6% | |
| Mtwara | 28.5% | |
| Arusha | 25.6% | |
| Kagera | 5.0% | Lowest — minimal redistricting |

**Policy relevance:** This kind of boundary reconciliation is essential for any longitudinal analysis of sub-national government data — elections, health outcomes, census linkages, or fiscal transfers.

## Part 2: Debiasing treatment effect estimates — Monte Carlo simulation

Designed a DGP with a **known treatment effect (β = 0.3 SD)**, then built 5 regression models varying which controls were included — confounder, mediator, and collider — running 200 simulations at each of 6 sample sizes.

**Key finding:** Only Model 2 (controlling for the confounder alone) recovers β = 0.30. A larger sample size *cannot fix structural bias* — Models 1 and 5 remain wrong at N=2,500.

| Model | Controls included | Bias direction | Recovers β = 0.30? |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1: Naive | None | Upward (~0.57) | ❌ |
| **M2: Correct** | **Confounder only** | **None** | **✅** |
| M3: + Mediator | Confounder + mediator | None (higher variance) | ✅ |
| M4: Collider | Confounder + collider | Downward (~0.15) | ❌ |
| M5: All | All covariates | Fully attenuated (~0.04) | ❌ |

**Collider warning:** Including a variable caused by both treatment and outcome introduces downward bias of ~0.15 — a counterintuitive and commonly overlooked error in applied policy research that *gets worse* with more data.

## Part 3: Spatial analysis — U.S. animal welfare enforcement choropleth

Merged a hand-coded **Animal Welfare Penalty Harshness Index** (0–100 scale, 48 states) with U.S. Census shapefiles in Stata using `spmap`, producing a choropleth revealing stark regional disparities in enforcement.

**Method:** `shp2dta` to import Census shapefiles → merge with penalty index → `spmap` with quantile color breaks → explicit handling of missing data (WY, SD) shown in legend as gray.

**Finding:** Northeast and Pacific Coast states score 75+; the Deep South and Mountain West cluster below 35 — consistent with broader regulatory divergence patterns in U.S. federalism.

```{=html}
<div class="proj-notebox">
  <div class="proj-notebox-title">Skills demonstrated</div>
  <div class="pills-row">
    <span class="pill pill-blue">Geospatial fuzzy matching</span>
    <span class="pill pill-blue">Shapefile processing</span>
    <span class="pill pill-sky">Panel data reconciliation</span>
    <span class="pill pill-sky">Stata bysort / merge</span>
    <span class="pill pill-teal">DAG reasoning</span>
    <span class="pill pill-teal">Collider / confounder identification</span>
    <span class="pill pill-slate">Choropleth mapping</span>
    <span class="pill pill-slate">ArcGIS · spmap</span>
  </div>
</div>
```
